coredata.dc:title: Effects of fungal carbon dots application on growth characteristics and cadmium uptake in maize
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2023 Elsevier Masson SAS. All rights reserved.
coredata.dc:creator: Sadeghi, Jalal
Lakzian, Amir
Halajnia, Akram
Alikhani, Mina
coredata.dc:description: The advancement of nanotechnology has led to the increased use of nanomaterials for the purpose of restoring contaminated soils. However, so far no research has been reported on the interactions of carbon dots with heavy metals (loid)s in phytoremediation. The purpose of this study was to investigate the effect of a new carbon dots derived from fungal exopolysaccharide (EPSs) on the growth and cadmium uptake in maize plants. This research was carried out using a completely randomized design with three replications in a greenhouse condition. Treatments included control, carbon dots (150 mg kg−1), cadmium (50 mg kg−1) and cadmium + carbon dots (50 mg kg−1+150 mg kg−1). The carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal method from EPSs. The results showed that shoot dry weight and chlorophyll content of maize increased 9.7% and 23.2% in the presence of carbon dots, respectively. Carbon dots improved the chlorophyll content of maize by 24.3% in the cadmium treatment. Cadmium concentration increased (106%) in maize shoot but it decreased in root maize (68%). Carbon dots caused an increase of 5.7 and 6.7 times in the transfer factor and phytoremediation rate of cadmium, respectively. The presence of carbon dots triggered an increase of 77.9% and 39.9% of dissolved organic carbon in non-contaminated and cadmium-contaminated soils, respectively. Soil microbial biomass carbon increased 54.9% and 24.1% carbon dots and cadmium + carbon dots treatments, respectively. The study demonstrates the potential of fungal carbon dots for phytoremediation of heavy metal (loid)s contaminated soils. It also highlights the potential of nanotechnology in environmental remediation efforts.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fugal carbon dots
Phytoremediation
Transfer factor
Phytoremediation rate

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Pearson's coefficient between soil properties and plants
Principal component analysis of treatment impact

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: We would like to acknowledge Ferdowsi University of Mashhad , Iran for funding this research (Project No: 55126 ).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of fungal carbon dots.
(a) Field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM) and (b) transmission electron microscopy (TEM) images of fungal carbon dots, whereas the inset shows the particle size distribution histogram.
The zeta potential value of fungal carbon dots.

The effect of experiment treatments on shoot and root dry weight of maize. Different letters show significant differences among treatments (P ≤ 0.05). Uppercase and lowercase letters indicate different statistical analysis. Standard deviation is shown by error bars (n = 3).

The effect of experiment treatments on shoot chlorophyll content and plant height of maize. Different letters show significant differences among treatments (P ≤ 0.05). Standard deviation is shown by error bars (n = 3).
The effect of experiment treatments on dissolved organic carbon of soil. Different letters show significant differences among treatments (P ≤ 0.05). Standard deviation is shown by error bars (n = 3).

The effect of experiment treatments on basal respiration and soil biomass carbon. Different letters show significant differences among treatments (P ≤ 0.05). Uppercase and lowercase letters indicate different statistical analysis. Standard deviation is shown by error bars (n = 3).

Pearson correlation coefficients between measured parameters. ShCC, RCC and SoCC represent the concentrations of cadmium in shoots, roots and soil, respectively. SDW and RDW represent the shoot and root dry weight, respectively. L and Ch represent plant height and shoot chlorophyll content, respectively. BR, DOC, TF, BAC, BCF and PR represent basal respiration, dissolved organic carbon, translocation factor, bioaccumulation coefficient, bioconcentration factor and phytoremediation ratio, respectively.

Relative Important Index of chemical and biological properties of soil and pollution indicators on shoot dry weight of maize. ShCC, RCC and SoCC represent the concentrations of cadmium in shoots, roots and soil, respectively. DOC, TF, BAC, BCF, PR, SBC and BR represent dissolved organic carbon, translocation factor, bioaccumulation coefficient, bioconcentration factor, phytoremediation ratio, soil biomass carbon and basal respiration respectively.

Principal component analysis (PCA) biplot of variables (black points) and responses (red arrows) for soil and plant properties. L, SDW, RDW and Ch. represent plant height, shoot and root dry weight and shoot chlorophyll content, respectively; ShCC, RCC and SoCC represent shoot, root and soil cadmium concentrations, respectively; SBC, BR. and DOC represent biomass carbon, soil respiration and dissolved organic carbon, respectively, and TF, BAC, BCF and PR represent the translocation factor, bioaccumulation coefficient, bioconcentration factor and phytoremediation ratio, respectively.
Variance analysis of the experimental effect of treatments on measured parameters.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: **and * are significant at the level of one (P ≤ 0.01) and five percent (P ≤ 0.05), respectively.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The effect of experimental treatments on cadmium concentration in shoot and root of maize and pollution indicators.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: Different letters show significant differences among treatments (P ≤ 0.05).

head.ce:title: Effects of fungal carbon dots application on growth characteristics and cadmium uptake in maize

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Soil Science, Faculty of Agriculture, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, 91779-48944, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Soil Science, Faculty of Agriculture, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Mashhad, 91779-48944, Iran

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, 19395-4697, Tehran, Iran
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Payame Noor University, 19395-4697, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The advancement of nanotechnology has led to the increased use of nanomaterials for the purpose of restoring contaminated soils. However, so far no research has been reported on the interactions of carbon dots with heavy metals (loid)s in phytoremediation. The purpose of this study was to investigate the effect of a new carbon dots derived from fungal exopolysaccharide (EPSs) on the growth and cadmium uptake in maize plants. This research was carried out using a completely randomized design with three replications in a greenhouse condition. Treatments included control, carbon dots (150 mg kg−1), cadmium (50 mg kg−1) and cadmium + carbon dots (50 mg kg−1+150 mg kg−1). The carbon dots synthesized by hydrothermal method from EPSs. The results showed that shoot dry weight and chlorophyll content of maize increased 9.7% and 23.2% in the presence of carbon dots, respectively. Carbon dots improved the chlorophyll content of maize by 24.3% in the cadmium treatment. Cadmium concentration increased (106%) in maize shoot but it decreased in root maize (68%). Carbon dots caused an increase of 5.7 and 6.7 times in the transfer factor and phytoremediation rate of cadmium, respectively. The presence of carbon dots triggered an increase of 77.9% and 39.9% of dissolved organic carbon in non-contaminated and cadmium-contaminated soils, respectively. Soil microbial biomass carbon increased 54.9% and 24.1% carbon dots and cadmium + carbon dots treatments, respectively. The study demonstrates the potential of fungal carbon dots for phytoremediation of heavy metal (loid)s contaminated soils. It also highlights the potential of nanotechnology in environmental remediation efforts.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Due to the rapid development of industries such as mining and automobile manufacturing, large amounts of heavy metals enter the environment (Du et al., 2019; Qiu and Gui, 2019). Heavy metals pollution in the environment has become an urgent problem that adversely affects plants, animals and human health. (Grytsyuk et al., 2006; Rezania et al., 2016). Cadmium (Cd) is one of the most important toxic environmental heavy metals. It is very toxic for all kind of life (Romero-Hernández et al., 2016). Many studies have been conducted to investigate the effects of cadmium on different crops. Anjum et al. (2015) stated that reactive oxygen species (ROS) and antioxidant activities increased in two different maize varieties and finally grain yield significantly decreased.

Various physical and chemical methods, such as precipitation, ion exchange, membrane separation and adsorption, are used to remove heavy metals from soils (Kumar et al., 2020). Phytoremediation also is a sustainable technology, using plants to directly extract heavy metal (loid)s from polluted soil, making it an environmentally friendly and cost-effective approach in soil remediation (Chen et al., 2021). Maize plant is considered as a suitable candidate for phytoremediation (Xu et al., 2013) because of its high biomass production. The results of studies showed that despite the absorption of cadmium in maize plant, the concentration of this metal in seeds was very low (Xu et al., 2013).

Nanomaterials (NMs) are materials that have a size of 100 nm or less. These materials have unique properties and are used in various industries such as electronics, manufacturing, medicine, and energy (Bianchi et al., 2011; Gong et al., 2017). Furthermore, NMs play a significant role in modern agriculture by providing controlled delivery of nutrients, fertilizers, and pesticides for plant growth and disease prevention (Gogos et al., 2012; Huang et al., 2018). The interaction between NMs and plants can influence the uptake of heavy metals by plants and their response to metal-induced toxicity (Gong et al., 2018; Rossi et al., 2018). However, there is limited research on the effects of NMs on the phytoremediation of heavy metal-contaminated sediments. Among different NMs, carbon-based nanomaterials, including carbon nanotubes (CNTs), graphene and carbon dots, etc are suitable adsorbents for removing heavy metals due to their specific surface area and many binding sites (Cao et al., 2006; Yusuf et al., 2015).

Many studies have shown that carbon nanomaterials have superior adsorption capacity and high adsorption efficiency for various heavy metals, such as lead, cadmium, nickel, copper, and chromium (Huang et al., 2020; Ii and Ii, 2020; Peng et al., 2020; Rebekah et al., 2020). Application of multi-walled carbon nanotube (MWCNTs) in soil has shown that three concentrations of 0.1, 0.5 and 1% of this compound have reduced the concentration of three forms of lead and copper (extractable with TCLP, extractable with calcium chloride and available), which shows the ability of nanomaterials to immobilize and stabilize these metals in the soil (Rizwan et al., 2016).

Carbon dots are zero-dimensional (0D) carbon-based nanomaterial with a diameter of less than 10 nm. They are broadly classified as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), carbon nanodots (CNDs), and carbonized polymeric dots (CPDs) (Liu et al., 2020). Carbon dots are optically active carbon-based nanomaterials with the intrinsic property of fluorescence. Their lateral widths are less than 10 nm, and their lattice spacing is between 0.21 and 0.34 nm. Most carbon dots comprise a sp2/sp3 hybridized carbon core with surface functional groups (Yahaya Pudza et al., 2020; Yarur et al., 2019). Carbon dots are classified based on their carbon core structure, surface functional groups, and characteristics. Their remarkable properties, which include simple synthesis methods, photoluminescence, biocompatibility, variable surface functionalities, water solubility, and low toxicity, have led to their widespread use in biological applications such as bioimaging, biosensing, photocatalysis, and molecule delivery (Feng et al., 2021; Mansuriya and Altintas, 2021). Also, carbon dots have been reported in many studies to improve photosynthesis (Wang et al., 2018a), nutrient absorption (Li et al., 2019), nitrogen-fixing (Wang et al., 2018b), total growth of plants (Li et al., 2018) and slow release Zn micronutrient (Alikhani et al., 2023).

Although carbon dots have been used to enhance crop plant growth, their effect on plant growth in the presence of cadmium toxicity has received little attention. The purpose of this study was to evaluate the effect of a fungal carbon dots on the growth characteristics and cadmium absorption in maize plants, as well as the biological aspects of a cadmium-contaminated calcareous soil.

Alternaria alternata fungus was obtained from Department of Plant Medicine, Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, recultured on Potato dextrose agar (PDA) and incubated for 72 h at 30 °C. The fungal biomass was placed into 250 mL Erlenmeyer flasks, each with 100 mL of culture medium (glucose, 40; yeast extract, 1.0; peptone, 0.5; KH2PO4; and MgSO4.7H2O, 0.5 kg m−3), and they were incubated for 10 day at 28 °C and 120 rpm. Finally, the exopolysaccharide produced will be extracted by ethanol (Dubois et al., 1956).

Carbon dots were synthesized from extracted exopolysaccharide using the one-step hydrothermal carbonization method (Lin et al., 2018). Fungal exopolysaccharide was dissolved in 50 mL of deionized distilled water to reach a final concentration of 500 mg/L, transferred to a 100 mL stainless steel autoclave with Teflon coating and then kept at 200 °C for 24 h. The obtained dark brown solution was cooled to room temperature and centrifuged at 13,000 rpm for 15 min. The supernatant was passed through a 0.22-μm filter membrane and then freeze-dried for further experiments.

The infrared spectra of KBr pellets were captured in the 400–4000 cm-1 range using a PerkinElmer FT-IR spectrophotometer at the standard ambient temperature. A transmission electron microscope (TEM) made by Zeiss and a Field Emission Scanning Electron Microscope (FESEM, ZEISS Company Sigma VP) were used to investigate the carbon dots morphology. The zeta potential of carbon dots was measured using a zeta analyzer (Zetasizer Malvern Company). Thermo Finnigan Flash 1112 EA was used to measure the element analyses (CHN).

This experiment was conducted in a completely randomized design with three replications under greenhouse conditions at Ferdowsi University of Mashhad. The treatments included control (without carbon dots and cadmium) (C), fungal carbon dots (150 mg kg−1) (CD), cadmium (50 mg kg−1) (Cd) and cadmium + carbon dots (50 mg kg−1+150 mg kg−1) (Cd + CD). There were twelve pots in total.

A soil sample was collected and air-dried from a research farm at Ferdowsi University of Mashhad at a depth of 0–30 cm. Basic physical and chemical properties were measured by conventional laboratory methods (Table 1 ). For cadmium treatment, cadmium chloride was applied to soil samples, and moisture of all soil samples was kept at 70 % of soil field capacity (FC) for two weeks. The soil samples were air-dried again and passed through a 4 mm sieve, and the required fertilizer (N, P and K) was added to the soil sample (100, 50, and 50 kg ha−1 of urea (CH4N2O), ammonium phosphate ((NH4)3PO4), and potassium nitrate (KNO3) were added and blended into each pot according to general guidelines). Carbon dots were dissolved in water and added to the soil in the first and fourth weeks after planting. The opening size and height of each pot were 20 and 18 cm, respectively, and it contained 5 kg of soil. Four single cross 704 maize seeds were planted in each pot. Two plants in each pot at the two-leaf stage were left. The plants were cultivated in a controlled environment, specifically a greenhouse, where the temperature fluctuated between 20 and 25 °C and received daily sunlight. The soil's humidity in the pot was regulated by weighing them each day to ensure it stayed at 70% of FC.

After two months, plants were harvested, and soil samples were collected. Then, different plant parts were manually separated, and plant height was measured. The roots and shoots were washed with distilled water and dried at 65 °C in the oven to a constant weight. The dry weight of shoot and root was calculated. The chlorophyll content of shoot was measured by (Lichtenthaler, 1987) method at two wavelengths of 649 nm (Chl a = 13.95 A665- 6.8 A649) and 665 nm (Chl b = 24.96 A649- 7.32 A665), and total chlorophyll was obtained from Chla + Chlb. After drying, the roots and shoots of plant were milled and digested by dry method (Vogt et al., 2015). The cadmium concentration in each plant parts was measured by an atomic absorption device (PG990). The total cadmium concentration in plant parts also calculated.

Three parameters of the transfer factor (TF) (Gonzaga et al., 2018), bioaccumulation factor (BAC) and bioconcentration factor (BCF) (Sidhu et al., 2018) and phytoremediation ratio (PR) (Embrandiri et al., 2017) were obtained using the following equations (Eq. (1) and (4)):
(Eq. 1)
TF =
C dshoot
C droot
(Eq. 2)
BAC =
C dshoot
C dsoil
(Eq. 3)
BCF =
C droot
C dsoil
(Eq. 4)
PR
( % )
=
Cadmium
concentration
in
plant × dry
biomass
of
plant × 100
Cadmium
concentration
in
soil × soil
weight
in
each
pot
Cd shoot is total cadmium concentration and Cd soil is available cadmium concentration.

Among the chemical and biological characteristics of soil, available cadmium concentration, dissolved organic carbon, microbial respiration and biomass carbon were measured. The amount of dissolved organic carbon (DOC) was measured by the method of Leaney (1992). Briefly, in this method, a soil suspension (1:10 ratio of soil/water) was prepared with deionized distilled water, shaken for 1 h, filtered and passed through a 0.46-μm filter, and dissolved organic carbon was measured by a TOC analyzer. Available soil cadmium was extracted by the DTPA-TEA method (Lindsay and Norvell, 1978) and measured by a PG990 atomic absorption device. Basal respiration and microbial biomass were also measured by the (Alef and Nannipieri, 1995) and (Sparling et al., 1988) methods, respectively.

Statistical analysis was performed using JMP software (JMP version 8). To analyze significant differences between treatments, one-way analysis of variance was used. The Tukey HSD test evaluated the comparison of treatment means at the 5% probability level. The index of relative importance between different parameters and the shoot dry weight was calculated by R software. Principal component analysis (PCA) of the measured parameters was performed using GraphPad Prism 9 software, and graphs were drawn by Excel (2016) and Origin 2022 software.

The FT-IR spectra of carbon dots (Fig. 1 ) showed the existence of hydroxyl (–OH) groups, amines (–NH2), and methylene groups (–CH2) at 3410, 3215, and 2950 cm−1, respectively (Yahaya Pudza et al., 2020). The peaks at 1690, 1590, and 1404 cm−1 correspond to the C=O stretching, C=C stretching in an aromatic ring, and bending vibration of CH2 groups, respectively (Atchudan et al., 2020). The peaks at 1080 and 1040 cm−1 were related to the asymmetric and symmetric stretching vibrations of epoxy (C–O–C) groups (Liu et al., 2014).
Elemental analysis (CHN) measured the exact percentage of C, H and N elements. The carbon, hydrogen and nitrogen contents of the prepared carbon dots were 16.99, 2.49, and 8.79%, respectively.

The FESEM and TEM images (Fig. 2 ) provided clear insights into the morphology of the carbon dots. The FESEM image (Fig. 2a) provided evidence of the amorphous nature of carbon, showing numerous pores present. Furthermore, by analyzing the particle size histogram, it was determined that the average particle size of the carbon dots was approximately 4 nm (inset of Fig. 2b).

The zeta potential value of carbon dots was measured to be −25.55 mV (Fig. 3 ). The value of zeta potential determines whether carbon dots are stable in the short or long term. Carbon dots with a high zeta potential (negative or positive) are considered electrostatically stable, whereas particles with a low zeta potential coagulate or aggregate quickly (Shishodia et al., 2021).

According to analysis of variance (ANOVA ((Table 2 ), the effect of experimental treatments on cadmium transport factor and microbial respiration was significant at the level of 5% (P ≤ 0.05) but for other parameters in was significant at the e level of 1% (P ≤ 0.01) (Table 2).

The concentration of cadmium in Cd and CD treatments was not at the level of detection. Cadmium concentration in maize root and availability of cadmium in soil decreased in carbon dots treatment. The cadmium concentration in maize root and soil in the Cd + CD treatment was reduced by 68% and 70.2%, respectively, compared to the Cd treatment (Table 3 ). The cadmium concentration in maize shoot increased by 106% in carbon dots treatment related to that in the Cd treatment.

BAC and BCF and translocation factor increased in carbon dots treatment. The values BAC, TF and PR increased 6.67, 5.72 and 6.7 times in the carbon dots treatment compared to the control, respectively. (Table 3). The BCF in the Cd + CD treatment increased by 19.3% compared to that in the control.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Pearson's coefficient between soil properties and plants
ce:section.ce:para: According to the results of Pearson's correlation coefficients, the parameters measured in Fig. 8 had the highest positive correlation values between PR and BAC (+0.99), and the highest negative correlation observed between shoot Cd and plant height of maize (−0.87). The shoot dry weight had the highest positive correlation with basal respiration (+0.92), and it had the highest negative correlation with cadmium concentration in shoot (−0.78). The amount of available soil cadmium had the highest negative correlation with shoot dry weight, microbial biomass carbon and plant chlorophyll content. The amount of dissolved organic carbon in the soil also had the highest positive correlation with three parameters of root dry weight, chlorophyll content and soil microbial biomass carbon. The correlation coefficients between soil microbial biomass carbon and basal respiration and dissolved organic carbon were + 0.82 and + 0.81, respectively.

The results of the relative importance index between the chemical and biological properties of soil with shoot dry weight indicated that the soil biological properties had a much more significant effect than the soil chemical properties on shoot dry weight. The results also showed that the highest relative importance index was between shoot dry weight and basal respiration and soil microbial biomass carbon. The relative importance index of dissolved organic carbon with shoot dry weight was also the lowest value (5.7%) among all the studied parameters (Fig. 9 ).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Principal component analysis of treatment impact
ce:section.ce:para: The PCA results showed that two principal components (PCs) could explain 78.85% of the total variation in the measured parameters (Fig. 10 ). The first principal component (PC1) represents 53.73% of the variability and mainly includes SoCC, TF, BAC, BCF, PR, DOC, Ch., BR. and BCF. PC2 explains 25.12% of the variation due to RCC and SoCC.

By examining the interaction of variables (black point) and responses (red arrows), it was determined that the effect of 50 mg kg−1cadmium (Cd) on RCC and SoCC, the effect of 150 mg kg−1 carbon dots (CD) on Ch., DOC, BCF, SDW and BR and the effect of simultaneous application of cadmium + carbon dots (Cd + CD) were positive on BAC, BCF and ShCC parameters (Fig. 10).

The prepared carbon dots have great potential for removal of cadmium metal from soil because of their porous structure, high surface area, nanoscale size, different functionalities, and negative surface charge (Baby et al., 2019).

Effect of cadmium on maize plant growth was negative, and shoot dry weight, chlorophyll content and plant height at 50 mg kg−1 cadmium decreased significantly compared to the control. The reduction of shoot dry weight in the presence of cadmium has also been reported by Michel-López et al. (2016), Okem et al. (2016). Vijendra et al. (2016) also reported that reactive oxygen species increased in cadmium treatment and because of that, shoot dry weight decreased. According to the results, a reduction in plant photosynthesis was observed that this finding was in agreement with the results of Haider et al. (2022) and Nazar et al. (2012). They reported that cadmium uptake caused the disturbance of water-plant relation and plant nutrients imbalance and then he photosynthesis and ultimately the shoot dry weight decreased significantly.

The results showed that the carbon dots had a beneficial impact on the growth characteristics of maize which is probably due to the increased photosynthetic activity (Tan et al., 2021) and facilitates the transporting water, nutrients, and photosynthates throughout the plant by carbon dots (Li et al., 2019a; Das et al., 2014). Researchers reported that carbon dots inhibited free radicals in plants because there were electron donor and acceptor of carboxyl functional groups on the surface of carbon dots (Zhao et al., 2015).

The Cd toxicity significantly reduced the chlorophyll content (Zhao et al., 2021). The result showed that the presence of carbon dots in Cd contaminated soil) had a positive effect on the chlorophyll content of maize plants. The addition of carbon dots improved the photosynthetic pigments, resulting in higher chlorophyll content. In addition, it is known that there are two energy conversion procedures taking place during plant photosynthesis: the conversion of light energy into electric energy and then into chemical energy (Haider et al., 2022). When carbon dots exposed to light enhanced charge transfer, leading to the formation of more electronic holes and thereby increasing photoelectric conversion efficiency (Lv et al., 2023). Moreover, carbon dots have fluorescence properties and when it is used in plants UV light transformed into blue light, thereby speeding up photosynthesis (Mitra et al., 2011).

Results showed that carbon dots increased Cd concentration maize shoot but it was reduced in maize root. This suggests that carbon dots promote the translocation of Cd from the roots to maize shoot. The presence of different functional groups and the negative charge of carbon dots (−25.55 mV) causes cadmium to be absorbed on the surface of carbon dots. There is currently a growing interest in the combination of nanotechnology and heavy metal-phytoremediation (Liu et al., 2021). Previous studies shown that nanomaterials can act as carriers for Cd, helping Cd uptake in plants nicotiana tabacum (Sai et al., 2018). Higher Cd translocation in maize plant is a by carbon dots is a valued feature in phytoremediation process (Sai et al., 2018). However, the exact mechanisms of translocation are still unclear and further research is needed to understand their influence on heavy metal translocation in plants. Chen et al. (2021) reported that cadmium concentration increased 75% and 30% in hydroponic culture in the presence of two types of functional and nonfunctional carbon dots (X. Chen et al., 2021). In Citrus maxima seedlings, at two levels of 50 and 500 mg kg−1 carbon dots, the concentration of cadmium in the root and aerial parts decreased (Li et al., 2019b).

The use of carbon dots increased TF, BAC and BCF and the values of these parameters were greater than one. These results generally showed a positive and significant effect of carbon dots on increasing the transfer of cadmium to maize. The reason for the decrease in the concentration of this heavy element in soil and plant roots is probably the increase in the transfer of this element to aerial organs in the presence of carbon dots. An increase in the cadmium translocation factor from 0.188 to 1.264 in the presence of carbon dots was an indication of the dominance of the phytoextraction phenomenon in the Cd + CD treatment compared to the Cd treatment (lower transfer factor values indicate dominance of the phytostabilization process).

Considering that the BCF value was greater than one in both the Cd and Cd + CD treatments, the root of maize is a suitable organ for bioremediation of cadmium by this plant. However, BCF and TF values greater than 1 in a plant indicate that this plant is a better candidate for removing metals from soil through phytoextraction (Mendez and Maier, 2008). Studies have shown that increasing the plant biomass and increasing the bioavailability of heavy metals in soil are among the appropriate solutions to improve the phytoextraction process of heavy metals (Adrees et al., 2015; Ashraf et al., 2019). In this study, according to the dry weight of shoots as well as the concentration of cadmium in soil, it seems that phytoextraction is the dominant phenomenon in the presence of carbon dots in the soil.

Despite the fact that the measured phytoremediation ratio values were below 1, carbon dots could result in a significant rise in this ratio. The development of biomass and the capacity to take up and store the metal are both necessary for a particular plant species to be able to remove heavy metals from the soil. Overall, this study's findings indicated that maize with carbon dots had a good capacity to remove cadmium from soil.

The presence of carbon dots increased soil DOC, basal respiration and soil microbial biomass carbon. The presence of cadmium caused a significant decrease in DOC. The studied soil microbiological indicators decreased with increasing cadmium concentration, which indicates the negative effect of cadmium on microbial metabolic processes and microbial growth (Liao et al., 2005). A negative correlation between the increase in cadmium concentration and the biological properties of soil was also reported by Cao et al. (2006), which is consistent with the results obtained in this study. However, the simultaneous application of carbon dots and cadmium decreased the rate of soil basal microbial respiration.

The soil DOC increased in carbon dots treatments which was not far from expected but soil DOC in the Cd + CD treatment reduced. Complexation of DOC with cadmium seems to be the main reason of uptake reduction. in maize. The decline in the soil availability Cd s another factor supporting this conclusion.

In this research, we investigated the possibility of using carbon dots derived from fungal exopolysaccharides to improve plant growth and aid in the removal of heavy metal pollution in soil. Our findings indicate that the incorporation of carbon dots into cadmium-contaminated soil led to enhancements in parameters such as shoot chlorophyll levels, plant height, and microbial biomass carbon. Furthermore, we observed positive effects of carbon dots on the levels of dissolved organic carbon and microbial biomass carbon in the soil. Additionally, carbon dots facilitated the transfer of cadmium and increased the rate at which phytoremediation occurred. These results indicate that carbon dots possess the potential to be a valuable amendment for enhancing phytoremediation efforts in soil contaminated with heavy metals. However, further investigation is required to determine the optimal quantities of carbon dots and their interaction with different concentrations of heavy metals. Overall, this study provides new insights into the application of nanomaterials as a means of environmental remediation and highlights the potential advantages of incorporating carbon dots into phytoremediation strategies.

All authors contributed to the study conception and design. Material preparation, data collection and analysis were performed by Jalal Sadeghi, Amir Lakzian, Akram Halajnia and Mina Alikhani. The first draft of the manuscript was written by Jalal Sadeghi and all authors commented on previous versions of the manuscript. All authors read and approved the final manuscript.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: We would like to acknowledge Ferdowsi University of Mashhad, Iran for funding this research (Project No: 55126).

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Mechanisms of silicon-mediated alleviation of heavy metal toxicity in plants: a review
Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf.

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Adrees, M., Ali, S., Rizwan, M., Zia-ur-Rehman, M., Ibrahim, M., Abbas, F., Farid, M., Qayyum, M.F., Irshad, M.K., 2015. Mechanisms of silicon-mediated alleviation of heavy metal toxicity in plants: a review. Ecotoxicol. Environ. Saf. 119, 186-197.
